title: Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA)
subtitle: 22 y/o male with type 1 diabetes, hyperglycemia and deep breathing
nursesNote: 1200 - 22 y/o male with type 1 diabetes brought to the ED by a friend. He ran out of insulin 3 days ago and has had a flu-like illness. He reports increasing thirst, frequent urination, nausea, and abdominal pain, and is now drowsy and confused. Breathing is deep and rapid, and the friend notes a 'fruity' smell to his breath. Mucous membranes are dry and skin turgor is poor. Fingerstick glucose reads 'HIGH'; the laboratory glucose is 540 mg/dL.

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select the FOUR (4) findings that BEST indicate diabetic ketoacidosis.

opts.text: Glucose 540 mg/dL with positive ketones
opts.rat: Marked hyperglycemia with elevated ketones is the biochemical hallmark of DKA.

opts.text: Deep, rapid (Kussmaul) respirations and a fruity breath odor
opts.rat: Kussmaul breathing blows off CO2 to compensate for metabolic acidosis, and the fruity odor reflects ketones.

opts.text: Low pH (7.18) and low bicarbonate (12)
opts.rat: A low pH with low bicarbonate confirms the metabolic acidosis of DKA.

opts.text: Dry mucous membranes, poor skin turgor, tachycardia, and low blood pressure
opts.rat: These reflect the significant dehydration from osmotic diuresis in DKA.

opts.text: Oxygen saturation of 98%
opts.rat: A normal oxygen saturation is reassuring but is not a sign of DKA.

opts.text: He is 22 years old
opts.rat: Age is background information, not a clinical cue for DKA.

opts.text: He has a friend at the bedside
opts.rat: This is not a clinical finding relevant to DKA.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) statements that correctly interpret this situation.

opts.text: Insulin deficiency caused hyperglycemia and fat breakdown into ketones, producing the acidosis
opts.rat: Without insulin, the body breaks down fat for fuel, generating ketoacids that cause the metabolic acidosis of DKA.

opts.text: The potassium may look normal or high now but total body potassium is depleted, and it will fall as insulin is given
opts.rat: Acidosis shifts potassium out of cells, so serum potassium can be high despite total-body depletion; insulin drives it back in, dropping serum levels-so potassium must be watched closely.

opts.text: Fluid replacement is a first priority because the client is severely dehydrated
opts.rat: Osmotic diuresis causes large fluid losses; IV isotonic fluids are started early to restore volume and perfusion.

opts.text: The best initial treatment is a single dose of subcutaneous long-acting insulin
opts.rat: Acute DKA is treated with IV fluids and a continuous IV regular insulin infusion, not a single dose of long-acting subcutaneous insulin.

opts.text: The deep, rapid breathing should be suppressed with sedation
opts.rat: Kussmaul respirations are a compensatory response to acidosis and must not be suppressed; treating the DKA resolves them.

opts.text: Fluids should be restricted to avoid overload
opts.rat: The client is volume-depleted; restricting fluids would worsen dehydration and perfusion.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.inst: Drag to rank the explanations from MOST likely (1) to LEAST likely (5).

opts.text: Diabetic ketoacidosis from missed insulin and illness
opts.rat: Missed insulin plus infection, with hyperglycemia, ketosis, and acidosis, makes DKA the clear diagnosis.

opts.text: Hyperosmolar hyperglycemic state (HHS)
opts.rat: A key differential, but HHS typically occurs in older type 2 clients with very high glucose and minimal ketones/acidosis-this client has marked ketoacidosis.

opts.text: Sepsis from the recent flu-like illness
opts.rat: Infection can precipitate DKA and must be evaluated, but it does not by itself explain the ketoacidosis picture.

opts.text: Hypoglycemia
opts.rat: The glucose is very high, not low, so hypoglycemia does not fit.

opts.text: A primary respiratory disorder causing the fast breathing
opts.rat: Least likely: the rapid breathing is compensating for a metabolic acidosis, not a primary lung problem.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: Select the FOUR (4) MOST appropriate interventions.

opts.text: Start IV isotonic fluid (0.9% normal saline) to restore volume
opts.rat: IV isotonic fluid resuscitation is a first priority to correct the severe dehydration in DKA.

opts.text: Begin a continuous IV regular insulin infusion per protocol
opts.rat: Regular insulin by continuous IV infusion lowers glucose and stops ketone production; only regular insulin is given IV.

opts.text: Monitor potassium closely and replace it as ordered as insulin is given
opts.rat: Because insulin drives potassium into cells, potassium must be monitored and replaced to prevent dangerous hypokalemia.

opts.text: Monitor glucose, electrolytes, and acid-base, and add dextrose to fluids when glucose approaches 200-250
opts.rat: Frequent monitoring guides therapy, and dextrose is added as glucose falls so the insulin infusion can continue clearing ketones without causing hypoglycemia.

opts.text: Give a large IV push of regular insulin without starting fluids
opts.rat: Fluids are prioritized, and insulin is given by controlled infusion; a large push without fluids is unsafe.

opts.text: Administer sodium bicarbonate routinely to all DKA clients
opts.rat: Bicarbonate is not given routinely; the acidosis resolves as fluids and insulin correct the underlying process.

opts.text: Withhold potassium entirely because the level is currently high
opts.rat: The level will fall with insulin; potassium is monitored and replaced rather than ignored.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Drag to rank the actions from FIRST (1) to LAST (5).

opts.text: Establish IV access and begin isotonic fluid resuscitation
opts.rat: FIRST-restoring circulating volume addresses the immediate threat of dehydration and poor perfusion.

opts.text: Verify the potassium level before/as the insulin infusion is started
opts.rat: SECOND-potassium must be checked because insulin is held if potassium is dangerously low until it is replaced.

opts.text: Start the continuous IV regular insulin infusion per protocol
opts.rat: THIRD-insulin lowers glucose and halts ketogenesis once fluids are running and potassium is addressed.

opts.text: Monitor glucose, potassium, and acid-base frequently and add dextrose as glucose falls
opts.rat: FOURTH-frequent monitoring and adding dextrose prevent hypoglycemia and hypokalemia during treatment.

opts.text: Provide diabetes teaching (sick-day rules, not stopping insulin) before discharge
opts.rat: LAST-education prevents recurrence once the client is stabilized.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: During treatment, classify each finding as Improving, Worsening, or Unchanged.

opts.text: Glucose falls steadily toward 250 and the pH rises toward normal
opts.rat: A controlled fall in glucose with a rising pH shows the DKA is resolving with treatment.

opts.text: Mental status clears and the Kussmaul breathing slows to a normal rate
opts.rat: Improving consciousness and normalizing respirations indicate the acidosis is correcting.

opts.text: Skin turgor and blood pressure normalize with fluids
opts.rat: Restored perfusion and turgor reflect successful volume replacement.

opts.text: Potassium drops to 2.9 mEq/L during the insulin infusion with new ECG changes
opts.rat: Hypokalemia developing as insulin drives potassium into cells is dangerous and requires holding insulin per protocol and replacing potassium.

opts.text: Glucose falls to 55 mg/dL because dextrose was not added to the fluids
opts.rat: Hypoglycemia from continuing insulin without adding dextrose is a preventable complication needing immediate treatment.

opts.text: The client develops a severe headache and decreasing responsiveness after rapid correction
opts.rat: New headache and declining consciousness raise concern for cerebral edema, a serious complication of overly rapid correction requiring urgent evaluation.

opts.text: Heart rate remains 118 early in resuscitation before much fluid has been given
opts.rat: A persistently elevated heart rate early in treatment reflects ongoing dehydration that has not yet been corrected.

opts.text: Oxygen saturation stays at 98%
opts.rat: A stable, normal oxygen saturation is neither an improvement nor a decline in this context.

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Physiological Adaptation, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
